items.title: ENGLAND TEAM PREVIEW 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿 | ANDY + FPL CHAI! | FIFA World Cup 2026 Fantasy Tips
items.headline: Kane leads England fantasy plans, but Konsa and O'Riley offer the value route
items.summary:
England enter the World Cup under Thomas Tuchel with strong fantasy appeal, even if the opening fixture against Croatia is not as explosive as some rival nations' Matchday 1 match-ups. The expected route is Croatia, Ghana and Panama in the group, with England fancied to go deep into the tournament, possibly at least the semi-finals, although a likely tougher knockout path and a potential Brazil quarter-final temper expectations. Tuchel's tournament pedigree and Harry Kane's career-best Bayern Munich form are central to the optimism.

The likely defensive shape has Jordan Pickford in goal, Reece James at right-back, Ezri Konsa and Marc Guehi at centre-back, and O'Riley at left-back. Pickford is viewed as reliable but not necessarily the best fantasy route, while James carries attacking upside through crossing to Kane but remains a minutes and fitness risk. Konsa, at 4.8m and around 1% owned, stands out because the game awards a two-point scouting bonus to sub-5% owned players who score more than four points. Guehi has set-piece threat, especially with Declan Rice's delivery, but Konsa's lower price and ownership make him the more attractive punt if friendlies confirm his place.

O'Riley is the preferred England defender. At 4.7m, he is the cheapest expected starter in the back line and brings major attacking upside after a breakout Premier League season under Pep Guardiola. His role under Tuchel needs monitoring, but if England form a back three in possession and allow him to attack the box, his aerial threat and goal potential make him the standout defensive pick. England's clean-sheet odds are solid rather than elite, with the panel expecting around two shutouts from Croatia, Ghana and Panama, but there is a case for waiting until Matchday 2 because Germany and Spain have stronger clean-sheet prospects in the opener.

Midfield is more complicated. Declan Rice and Elliot Anderson are likely secure for starts and minutes, but the game's scoring leans heavily towards attacking output, making them less exciting unless Rice's set pieces deliver. Bukayo Saka is the safest starter among the advanced midfielders but costs 9.5m and may be protected around the 60-70 minute mark after an injury-hit season. Morgan Rogers is backed as a possible starter ahead of Jude Bellingham, though the battle is close and friendlies are crucial; Bellingham becomes interesting if Tuchel clearly trusts him, but rotation risk remains. Marcus Rashford could start on the left ahead of Anthony Gordon, yet his minutes also look vulnerable.

Kane is the clear top England pick at 10.5m and over 40% ownership. He is on penalties, plays through the centre of everything England do, benefits from shots-on-target scoring and is in Ballon d'Or-level form. The main debate is opportunity cost: Germany, Spain, France and Norway have forwards with easier Matchday 1 fixtures and far higher projected team goals, so Kane is not an automatic opener for every strategy. Holding him through Matchdays 1 and 2 is viable, but if England win their first two games, the Panama fixture could bring heavy rotation and even an Ollie Watkins start, making a Matchday 3 wildcard or transfer plan important.
key_points: Harry Kane is England's No. 1 fantasy pick: 10.5m, penalty taker, elite form and viable from Matchday 1, though not necessarily the best Matchday 1 captain over forwards from Germany, Spain, France or Norway.
Ezri Konsa is the best differential defender if he starts: 4.8m, around 1% owned and eligible for the two-point sub-5% scouting bonus if he scores more than four points.
O'Riley is the preferred England defensive asset at 4.7m because of his attacking and aerial upside, but his exact role under Tuchel needs checking in the friendlies.
Reece James offers high ceiling through crossing and attacking quality, but fitness and 60-minute risk make him a wait-and-see option rather than a lock.
England's fixtures are Croatia, Ghana and Panama; Croatia is the trickiest opener, while Ghana and especially Panama are the better attacking entry points.
Rice and Anderson look like the safest midfield minutes, but Rice's set pieces are the main reason to consider either because defensive midfielders are not heavily rewarded.
Saka is nailed but expensive at 9.5m and may be managed for minutes; Rogers, Bellingham, Rashford and Gordon are all more dependent on friendly line-ups and Tuchel's preferences.
If England win their first two matches, Matchday 3 rotation is a major risk, with Ollie Watkins a possible Panama punt if Kane is rested.

items.title: How to play World Cup Fantasy? - Differences compared to FPL! 🤔 #worldcup2026
items.headline: In-play substitutions make strong benches essential in World Cup Fantasy 2026
items.summary:
World Cup Fantasy 2026 launches with several major differences from Fantasy Premier League, and managers need to adjust quickly. The scoring system is broader than FPL: midfielders gain points for tackles and key passes, forwards are rewarded for shots on target, and players earn points for winning penalties even if the spot-kick is missed. Free-kick goals also carry an extra reward, while defenders and goalkeepers score more heavily for clean sheets but are punished more for conceding goals.

The game also replaces the familiar FPL chip landscape with a new set of tournament-specific tools. The Wildcard remains, but four additional chips change strategic planning: Maximum Captain automatically gives the armband to the highest-scoring player in the starting XI, 12th Man adds an extra free player to the squad, Qualification Booster rewards players who reach the next knockout round, and a mystery chip is still to be revealed.

The biggest strategic shift is in-play management. Unlike FPL, World Cup Fantasy allows managers to remove a player who has already played and bring in a bench player whose match has not yet started. That makes a strong bench far more important than in a standard FPL setup, because low scorers can be replaced across the matchday rather than locked in permanently.

Captaincy also becomes a “stick or twist” decision. If an early captain performs well, managers can keep the points; if the score disappoints, they can sacrifice those captain points and move the armband to a later player who has not yet played. Success in World Cup Fantasy 2026 therefore depends less on a fixed XI and more on planning match order, bench strength, captaincy ladders and chip timing around the tournament schedule.
key_points: Midfielders score for tackles and key passes, while forwards gain points for shots on target.
Winning a penalty earns points even if the penalty is not converted.
Defenders and goalkeepers are rewarded more for clean sheets but penalised more for goals conceded.
A two-point scouting bonus is available for selecting players under 5% ownership.
New chips include Maximum Captain, 12th Man, Qualification Booster and a still-unrevealed mystery chip, alongside the Wildcard.
In-play substitutions allow managers to replace poor starters with bench players who have not yet played.
Captaincy can be switched from an already-played captain to a later player, turning each matchday into a stick-or-twist decision.
Building a strong bench is essential because substitutes are active tactical options, not just injury cover.

items.title: SPAIN TEAM PREVIEW 🇪🇸 | ANDY + FPL GENERAL! | FIFA World Cup 2026 Fantasy Tips
items.headline: Oyarzabal emerges as Spain’s safest route into dream opening fixtures
items.summary:
Spain enter the 2026 World Cup as one of the tournament favourites, with expectations of at least a semi-final or final built around a young, high-upside squad led by Lamine Yamal, Pedri and a strong midfield core. The opening schedule is the major fantasy draw: Cape Verde in Matchday 1 on June 15, Saudi Arabia in Matchday 2 on June 21, then Uruguay in Matchday 3. The first two fixtures look tailor-made for attacking points, but Spain also carry one of the biggest rotation risks in the game because several key players are managing fitness issues and the squad has enough depth to win those early matches without overextending stars.

Mikel Oyarzabal is the standout Spain pick. At 8.1m, he looks likely to start up front, is expected to be on penalties, has chance-creation routes to points and can score direct free-kicks. With Lamine Yamal and Nico Williams both injury concerns for Matchday 1, Oyarzabal’s security improves further, and he is even preferred over some premium forwards such as Harry Kane, Erling Haaland, Cristiano Ronaldo and Kai Havertz for the opening round because Spain’s fixture against Cape Verde has greater haul potential. Kylian Mbappe remains the premium forward lock, but Oyarzabal is viewed as close to essential alongside him.

The midfield recommendation centres on Pedri at 8.1m. Although Fabian Ruiz is cheaper at 6.8m and currently low-owned enough to qualify for the scouting bonus if he stays under 5%, Pedri’s set pieces, open-play creativity and recent Barcelona form make him the preferred Spanish midfielder. Rodri is less attractive without defensive contribution scoring, and both he and Ruiz may have managed minutes because of recent injuries and season workload. Mikel Merino is also worth monitoring after scoring six goals in qualifying, but he is only just returning from injury, while Gavi and Dani Olmo add further competition and rotation uncertainty.

In defence, Marc Cucurella is the preferred pick despite his 18.5% ownership removing any scouting-bonus upside. He appears the most secure full-back, plays advanced for Spain and should benefit from matches where Spain dominate territory. Unai Simon is the first-choice goalkeeper rather than David Raya, but a Spanish defensive slot may be less appealing than an attacking one unless managers want clean-sheet coverage. Aymeric Laporte is worth watching if his ownership remains below 5%, while Pedro Porro would become highly attractive if friendlies suggest he starts over Marcos Llorente at right-back because of his attacking and set-piece threat.

The main caution is Lamine Yamal. Despite ownership near 47%, he is a risky Matchday 1 pick because Spain do not need to force him back against Cape Verde, and the fantasy deadline arrives four days before Spain play. A better route may be to avoid him initially and use the 12th Man chip on him in Matchday 2 if his fitness is clearer. Nico Williams carries similar risk, while Ferran Torres could be a high-upside differential captain only if reliable team news confirms he starts on the wing.
key_points: Top Spain pick: Mikel Oyarzabal at 8.1m, likely starter, penalty taker and strong captain option against Cape Verde in Matchday 1.
Pedri is preferred in midfield thanks to set pieces, creativity and goal threat, despite Fabian Ruiz offering cheaper under-5% scouting-bonus potential.
Avoid Lamine Yamal in initial drafts unless fitness news improves; he is a better Matchday 2 12th Man candidate than a risky Matchday 1 starter.
Nico Williams is also an injury/managed-minutes concern, while Ferran Torres and Dani Olmo become differential routes only if team news points to starts.
Marc Cucurella is the favoured defensive pick, with attacking positioning and strong clean-sheet prospects in the first two matches.
Unai Simon is Spain’s first-choice goalkeeper, not David Raya, though pairing him with a lower-owned earlier-playing keeper could be a safer strategy.
Watch friendlies for the right-back battle: Pedro Porro has big fantasy upside, but Marcos Llorente currently looks more likely to start.
Spain’s best fantasy window is Matchdays 1-2 against Cape Verde and Saudi Arabia; Matchday 3 against Uruguay brings tougher opposition and likely rotation.

items.title: HOW TO PLAY WORLD CUP FANTASY! | Interactive Guide, New Rules & Pro Tips for World Cup 2026 🏆
items.headline: World Cup 2026 Fantasy rewards active managers with manual subs, captain switches and differential bonuses
items.summary:
World Cup 2026 Fantasy gives managers a $100m budget to build a 15-man squad: two goalkeepers, five defenders, five midfielders and three forwards, with a maximum of three players per nation in the group stage. Only the starting XI scores unless a benched player auto-subs in for a non-playing starter, but the game strongly rewards building a full squad rather than just an elite XI because manual substitutions and captain switches can be made during each match day while fixtures are still unfolding.

Scoring differs sharply from FPL. Goalkeepers and defenders earn five points for a clean sheet but are punished heavily for conceding multiple goals, making weak defensive picks risky. Defenders get seven points for goals, midfielders get six and forwards get five, while midfielders gain extra points for tackles and chances created, and forwards gain extra points for shots on target. That boosts the appeal of creative midfielders such as Bruno Fernandes, Raphinha and Michael Olise, as well as high-volume shooters like Erling Haaland, Cristiano Ronaldo and Harry Kane. A major wrinkle is the scouting bonus: any player under 5% ownership at the deadline earns an extra two points if they score at least four points in that match, rewarding managers who find genuine differentials.

The tournament is split into eight match days, from the three group-stage rounds through the round of 32, round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals, and the final plus third-place playoff. Managers get unlimited transfers before Matchday 1 and again before Matchday 4 after the group stage, with two free transfers for Matchdays 2 and 3, then four before Matchdays 5 and 6, five before Matchday 7 and six before Matchday 8. Extra transfers cost three points each, prices do not change during the tournament, and nation limits rise as the field narrows: four players per country in Matchdays 4-5, five in the quarter-finals, six in the semi-finals and eight for Matchday 8.

The biggest edge comes from in-play management. The optimal structure is to start players whose teams play early in a match day and leave later-playing assets on the bench, allowing underperformers to be manually removed before the substitutes play. The same principle applies to captaincy: start with an early fixture, then twist to later options if the score is not good enough. A rough threshold is to stick on 12+ points early in a match day, around 10 in the middle, and eight or fewer near the end depending on remaining options. Once a manual sub is made, automatic substitutions are disabled, and once a captaincy or substitution decision is made away from a player who has already played, it cannot be reversed.

There are five boosters, with four confirmed: Maximum Captain automatically doubles the highest scorer in the squad for that match day; 12th Man adds one uncaptainable player with no budget or nation-limit restriction; Wild Card allows unlimited transfers in a non-unlimited-transfer round; and Qualification Booster adds two points to each starting XI player whose nation progresses, available only from Matchday 4 onward. The fifth booster remains a mystery until later in the tournament, so early planning should focus on using the known chips around fixture density, knockout progression and squad repair needs.
key_points: Build a strong 15-man squad, not just a starting XI, because manual substitutions let later-playing bench players replace poor early scorers.
Captain early-playing assets first, then switch if needed; sensible stick thresholds are roughly 12+ points early, 10 mid-matchday and around eight late on.
Creative midfielders benefit from chances created and tackles, making players like Bruno Fernandes, Raphinha and Michael Olise especially attractive under the scoring system.
High-volume forwards such as Erling Haaland, Cristiano Ronaldo and Harry Kane gain extra value from the shots-on-target scoring rule.
The scouting bonus gives two extra points to sub-5% owned players who score at least four points, making low-owned differentials unusually valuable.
Transfers are unlimited before Matchday 1 and Matchday 4; extra transfers beyond the free allocation cost three points, and player prices do not change.
Nation limits rise from three players per country in the group stage to four in Matchdays 4-5, five in Matchday 6, six in Matchday 7 and eight in Matchday 8.
Boosters include Maximum Captain, 12th Man, Wild Card and Qualification Booster, with the Qualification Booster only usable from the round of 32 onward.

items.title: HOW I FINISHED TOP 1,000 🔥 FPL 2025/26 SEASON REVIEW
items.headline: Bruno Fernandes captaincy seals a 588th-place FPL finish after late Gameweek 38 chaos
items.summary:
A 68-point Gameweek 38 delivers a final green arrow from 926th to 588th overall, securing a first top-1,000 Fantasy Premier League finish after 16 seasons. The decisive final-week swing comes with plenty of fortune: Erling Haaland to Jarrod Bowen goes through just before the deadline, but an attempted Bukayo Saka to Mohamed Salah move fails as the site struggles. That leaves Bruno Fernandes as captain instead of Salah, and his goal, assist and bonus points become a 28-point captaincy haul. Bowen adds 12 points, Antoine Semenyo scores, Trent Alexander-Arnold returns 10, and late defensive contribution points for James Hill help push the final rank inside the top 600.

The season review shows how central reliable, nailed players are to the success. Haaland is the most-captained player and dominates the first half of the campaign with repeated double-digit returns. Fernandes is the most-started player and becomes the defining pick of the season, scoring heavily from a role that combines strong underlying numbers, penalties, set pieces and defensive contributions. Arsenal defensive assets David Raya and Gabriel also prove crucial, with Raya finishing as the top-scoring goalkeeper and Gabriel combining clean sheets, goal threat, bonus and defensive contribution points.

The most-used squad core reflects a low-maintenance, reliability-first approach: Raya, Gabriel, Virgil van Dijk, Sessi, Anderson, Fernandes, Saka, Semenyo, Kiernan Dewsbury-Hall, Haaland and Thiago. Dewsbury-Hall works as a cheap nailed Everton number 10, Semenyo becomes a major asset across Bournemouth and Manchester City, and Thiago emerges as a strong value forward after arriving as Ivan Toney’s replacement. The defence is deliberately left largely untouched between the Gameweek 4 and Gameweek 32 wildcards, with most defensive transfers focused on Arsenal because of their elite clean-sheet potential.

The main lessons for 2026/27 are clear: accept luck as part of FPL, avoid tilting after bad outcomes, prioritise nailed minutes, and do not chase short-term form too aggressively. Defensive contribution points are now a major part of squad building, though their impact depends on next season’s prices; players like Elliot Anderson, Gabriel and Sessi are likely to rise. Rolling transfers remains underrated because banking two, three or more moves creates flexibility for structural changes without hits. There is also a case for FPL to improve forwards, with midfielders currently benefiting from extra goal points, clean-sheet points and more frequent defensive contribution upside.
key_points: Final rank is 588th overall after a 68-point Gameweek 38, rising from 926th and securing a first top-1,000 finish.
Gameweek 38 luck is decisive: Haaland to Bowen succeeds, Saka to Salah fails, and Bruno Fernandes remains captain for a 28-point haul.
Jarrod Bowen scores 12 points in the final week, while Trent Alexander-Arnold adds 10 and James Hill gains late defensive contribution points.
Bruno Fernandes is the season’s key asset, combining nailed minutes, penalties, set pieces, strong underlying numbers and defensive contributions.
David Raya and Gabriel are major long-term wins, with Arsenal’s defence trusted heavily and very few defender transfers made between wildcards.
Defensive contribution players are central to the strategy, but likely price rises for assets such as Elliot Anderson, Gabriel and Sessi may reduce their value next season.
Rolling transfers is viewed as underrated because multiple free moves allow bigger restructuring without hits; no hits are taken all season.
Bench Boost in Gameweek 1 is under consideration for 2026/27 to remove the chip early, while more early-season differential risk is also on the table.

items.title: FPL 25/26 SEASON REVIEW! 🔙 26K Rank..! Mavropanos Benched 😂 | Fantasy Premier League Tips 2025/26
items.headline: Mavropanos benching caps a 26k finish and a DEFCON-heavy FPL season
items.summary:
The FPL 2025/26 season ends on a green arrow but with a painful final-day error. Gameweek 38 brings 67 points against a safety score of 58 and a final rank of around 26k, ending a five-season top-10k streak. Bruno Fernandes is the key success as captain with 28 points, while Mohamed Salah returns an assist after coming in. Jared Bowen also proves a strong transfer after early Manchester City news shows Erling Haaland is not starting, but the late scramble backfires elsewhere: Omar Marmoush is bought on the assumption he starts up front and then stays unused, while Mavropanos scores eight points but remains third on the bench because the team is not reordered before deadline. Van Hecke starts instead, costing seven points and a possible top-25k finish.

The wider season is still consistent, but the route to 26k leaves clear lessons. The usual rank targets are broadly hit — top 1m by Gameweek 10, top 200k by Gameweek 20 and top 50k by Gameweek 30 — but each comes late, leaving too much to do in the final stretch. The chip period does not deliver the normal late surge: the Gameweek 32 wildcard brings a red arrow, the Gameweek 33 bench boost is excellent with 128 points, and the Gameweek 34 free hit barely moves rank. The Gabriel triple captain is also judged a mistake, even if Haaland later fails to punish it heavily. Rolling transfers too often late in the season also reduces aggression and flexibility.

The biggest regret comes around Gameweek 18. Early price-change moves bring in Ekitike and Declan Rice while selling Thiago and Bukayo Saka, before a rare Bruno Fernandes injury forces a further move to Matheus Cunha. The damage compounds when Saka is bought back by selling Antoine Semenyo, who then explodes after joining Manchester City, while Thiago immediately hits multiple hauls after being sold and then blanks after being bought back. Late-season calls also hurt: buying Gyokeres and selling Semenyo are flagged as two of the clearest errors, while Morgan Gibbs-White or holding Elliot Anderson may have been better routes.

The main strategic lesson is the rise of defensive contributions. A 3-4-3 is used more than any other formation, but 4-4-2 and 3-5-2 score better, largely because they make room for reliable DEFCON defenders and midfielders rather than chasing volatile forward goals. Too many points are benched from defenders and defensive midfielders such as James Hill, Chris Richards and Elliot Anderson, with ceiling picks repeatedly preferred over safer floors. Looking ahead to FPL 2026/27, centre-backs and DEFCON midfielders need higher prices, Haaland and Bruno Fernandes should be expensive enough to force a real premium choice, forwards may need five points per goal, and defensive contributions would work better as a tiered system rather than a flat two-point reward.
key_points: Final rank is around 26k after a 67-point Gameweek 38 green arrow, ending a five-year run of top-10k finishes.
Bruno Fernandes captaincy is the Gameweek 38 success, returning 28 points; Salah and Bowen are useful late transfers.
The major final-day mistake is leaving Mavropanos third on the bench after buying him, losing seven net points versus starting Van Hecke.
Late Manchester City team news leads to Bowen replacing Haaland successfully, but Marmoush is wrongly bought as a presumed striker starter and stays unused.
Chip execution is mixed: Gameweek 32 wildcard red arrow, Gameweek 33 bench boost 128 points, Gameweek 34 free hit effectively flat.
Worst transfer sequence comes around Gameweek 18: selling Saka and Thiago, Bruno’s injury forcing Cunha, then selling Semenyo just before his Manchester City scoring run.
DEFCON players are the season’s major lesson, with 4-4-2 and 3-5-2 outperforming 3-4-3 because reliable defenders and defensive midfielders beat volatile forwards.
For FPL 2026/27, DEFCON defenders and midfielders should be priced higher, Haaland and Bruno should force a premium decision, and forwards may deserve five points per goal.

items.title: FPL GW38 DEADLINE STREAM ✅ HAALAND CHERKI O'REILLY BENCHED ❌
items.headline: Haaland benching blows up Gameweek 38 as Bowen and Salah become late targets
items.summary:
Gameweek 38 becomes a rotation minefield, with late team news driving almost every decision. Bukayo Saka is treated as a priority sell or bench because Arsenal have the league wrapped up and a PSG final next Sunday, making a start feel highly unlikely. The same logic puts several Arsenal assets at risk, though Gabriel is seen as more likely to start than Declan Rice, while defenders such as Gabriel or Saliba are considered reasonable holds because they may either start or not feature at all.

The early plan is Saka to Cole Palmer, with Morgan Gibbs-White and Dominik Szoboszlai also highlighted as strong midfield punts. Palmer is preferred for upside if Chelsea finish strongly against Sunderland and link with João Pedro, while Gibbs-White appeals because his recent underlying numbers are excellent and Bournemouth are poor away. Salah is viewed as the premium Gameweek 38 punt if affordable, especially after news that he starts alongside Andy Robertson.

Captaincy initially centres on Erling Haaland if he starts against a likely rotated Aston Villa side, but falling Manchester City goal odds raise concern before the decisive leak arrives: Haaland, Nico O'Reilly and Rayan Cherki do not start. That forces a late pivot away from triple City. Jarrod Bowen becomes the preferred captain, ahead of Bruno Fernandes and Salah, because West Ham have huge motivation against Leeds and Bowen offers high upside in a fixture that could decide relegation. Bruno is still a strong option, but Brighton away is considered less appealing.

The final move is aggressive by Gameweek 38 standards: Haaland to Bowen and Trossard to Salah for a minus four, with Bowen captained and Salah vice-captained. The hit is justified by the number of non-starters already in the squad and the risk of failing to field 11 players. The main remaining trade-off is whether to start Saka for a possible cameo over a low-upside defender, but the core late call is clear: trust the leaks, move off benched City assets, and attack the final day with Bowen and Salah.
key_points: Late reliable leaks say Haaland, Nico O'Reilly and Rayan Cherki do not start for Manchester City; Salah and Robertson start for Liverpool.
Final transfers are Haaland to Bowen and Trossard to Salah for a minus four, made to avoid being short of starters.
Bowen is the preferred captain after the Haaland benching, with Salah vice-captain; Bruno Fernandes is respected but Brighton away lowers his appeal.
Saka is considered very unlikely to start because Arsenal have the PSG final next Sunday, making him a sell or bench rather than a starter.
Palmer, Gibbs-White and Szoboszlai are the main Saka replacement options if not stretching to Salah; Gibbs-White has the best recent underlying numbers.
West Ham vs Leeds is targeted for upside and motivation, with Bowen backed to deliver in a relegation-deciding fixture.
Bench Boosting in Gameweek 38 is strongly discouraged in principle because mass rotation makes it the worst week of the season for reliable starts.
Free Hit thinking shifts away from Haaland and toward a midfield-heavy side featuring Bowen, Salah, Bruno, Palmer and Gibbs-White, with cheap bench cover.

items.title: FPL 2025/26 END OF SEASON KNEEJERK STREAM 🔥
items.headline: Bruno Fernandes captaincy caps top-1k finish as GW38 chaos turns lucky
items.summary:
The season ends with a likely top-1k overall finish after a chaotic Gameweek 38 in which the FPL site crash changes the final decision path. The planned late move is Saka to Mohamed Salah, with Salah set to take the captaincy, but the transfer does not go through. That leaves Bruno Fernandes as captain, and he outscored Salah, turning a frustrating deadline failure into a major rank boost. The final score sits around 66 points once autosubs and bonus are applied, with Bowen arriving for Haaland, Tavernier and Semenyo returning, and one free transfer ultimately rolled.

The final-day transfer thinking is framed around avoiding an unnecessary hit. Marmoush is considered, but only becomes a serious option if Semenyo is confirmed benched and the Arsenal news makes it worthwhile; without that certainty, the move is not taken. Mavropanos and Pedro Porro are the defensive targets for those chasing Gameweek 38 upside, and both deliver clean sheets. There is still a note of caution for tight mini-leagues: Bruno’s assist for Dorgu’s goal may depend on final Opta/FPL review, and any change could affect both his points and bonus.

The main season lesson is clear: prioritise players who are absolutely nailed for minutes. That approach allows transfers to be rolled, reduces the need for hits and creates flexibility when injuries or fixture swings arrive. This is the first season completed without taking a hit, helped by extra transfers and chip structure, but also by consistently avoiding rotation-prone punts. The success is not presented as pure skill either; luck is openly acknowledged, especially in the final week and in key moments such as single-gameweek captaincy returns.

Early thoughts for next season point towards defensive contribution points staying, with only minor tweaks likely, while double chips may remain and Assistant Manager is unlikely to return. A Gameweek 1 Bench Boost is firmly on the table after the stress of trying to set it up later this season. Bruno Fernandes is expected to be priced around £10.0m-£10.5m rather than £12.0m despite a 230-plus point campaign, Haaland remains the obvious top premium if Salah is no longer central, and Chelsea wing-backs become highly attractive if Xabi Alonso uses a back three with no European distractions.
key_points: Gameweek 38 ends on roughly 66 points and a likely top-1k finish, with Bruno Fernandes captain, Bowen in for Haaland, and Tavernier/Semenyo returns.
The intended final transfer is Saka to Salah with Salah captain, but the site crash blocks it; Bruno captaincy proves more profitable.
A Marmoush hit is considered but likely avoided unless Semenyo’s benching and Arsenal team news make the upside clear.
Bruno’s assist for Dorgu’s goal may still be reviewed; any Opta/FPL correction could affect bonus points and tight mini-leagues.
Mavropanos and Pedro Porro are the preferred Gameweek 38 defensive punts, and both reward the call with clean sheets.
The season strategy lesson is to buy nailed starters, roll transfers where possible and avoid hits; this is the first no-hit season completed.
Defensive contribution points are expected to stay next season, double chips may remain, and a Gameweek 1 Bench Boost is a serious option.
Early next-season watchlist: Bruno around £10.0m-£10.5m, Haaland as the main premium, cautious Palmer pricing, and Chelsea wing-backs if Alonso plays a back three.

items.title: EARLY TEAM NEWS? 🕵️ FPL GW38 LIVE DEADLINE STREAM 📸  | Fantasy Premier League 2025/26
items.summary: {"headline":"Haaland benching blows up GW38: Salah and Bowen become late rescue buys","summary":"Game

items.title: FPL GW38 EXPERTS TEAM ✅ FINAL VIDEO! 🫡
items.headline: Bowen and Haaland lead Gameweek 38 as West Ham fight survival and Pep gets his send-off
items.summary:
Gameweek 38 arrives with most chips already spent and the top all-time managers largely focused on one or two final transfers. Expert managers average 61 points in Gameweek 37 against a global average of 53, and 21 of the tracked managers sit inside the top 10k going into the finale. A small minority even plan to roll a transfer, despite transfers not carrying into next season, with the only tiny benefit being that fewer total transfers can decide rank ties on equal points.

Jarrod Bowen is the dominant buy, with 35% bringing him in for West Ham’s must-win home fixture against Leeds. Leeds are injury-hit, have nothing left to play for, and have just allowed Brighton more than three expected goals, while West Ham need victory and a Spurs defeat to stay up. Pedro Porro and Richarlison are the main Spurs punts at home to Everton, while Konstantinos Mavropanos is another defender option for those backing West Ham. Dominik Szoboszlai is also popular before Liverpool host Brentford, and Anthony and Josh Brownhill/Flemming-style cheap punts attract interest in Burnley v Wolves, a fixture between two of the league’s weakest defences.

The main sales are Viktor Gyokeres, Joao Pedro, Rayan Cherki, Bukayo Saka, Marcus Tavernier and Dominic Calvert-Lewin. Arsenal are particularly awkward: the title is secured, a Champions League final against PSG is coming, and Mikel Arteta could protect key players such as Saka, although there is also a case for going strong to maintain rhythm. Manchester City look slightly more reliable than usual because it is Pep Guardiola’s final home match, Aston Villa have just won the Europa League and may lack intensity, but early substitutions remain a real risk.

Captaincy is led by Erling Haaland on 57%, followed by Bruno Fernandes on 27%, Bowen on 11%, Saka on 3%, plus one-off punts on Salah and Flemming. The logic is narrative-heavy but plausible: Gameweek 38 tends to be high-scoring, Haaland has Villa at home in Guardiola’s farewell, Bruno is chasing the assist record, and Bowen should be nailed for 90 minutes with set pieces and penalties in a survival match. Salah is a sentimental differential in what is framed as his Liverpool farewell.

The final expert squad is Verbruggen; O’Riley, Gabriel, Van Hecke; Saka, Cherki, Bruno Fernandes, Tavernier; Haaland, Bowen and Calvert-Lewin, with Darlow, Hill, Gross and Strakosha benched. Saka remains because sales are spread across replacements such as Szoboszlai, Palmer and Anthony. The wider lesson from the late-season wildcard and bench boost is cautionary: chasing double gameweeks brought upside, but double Brighton defence, Tavernier and Gross have disappointed while steady Arsenal defensive picks have delivered four straight clean sheets. The best Gameweek 38 approach is to wait for team leaks, map several scenarios in advance, keep at least one bench option, and be ready for a late move or even a hit if key players such as Haaland, Cherki, Almiron or Saka are benched.
